title: Using genomics to track environmental virus transmission and evolution
keywords: dust/air sampling (clinic waiting area; passive air sampler)
dust accumulation experiments under varied environmental conditions
phone dust accumulation experiment with/without charging (sequencing ongoing)

summary.hook: This poster describes three applications of unbiased metagenomic sequencing and bioinformatics to detect viruses and infer evolutionary history and transmission routes across environmental matrices, including mosquito pools, dust/air samples, and dust accumulation on electronic devices.
summary.summary_30s: This poster describes three applications of unbiased metagenomic sequencing and bioinformatics to detect viruses and infer evolutionary history and transmission routes across environmental matrices, including mosquito pools, dust/air samples, and dust accumulation on electronic devices. The mosquito surveillance work (in collaboration with Maricopa County Vector Control and ASU collaborators) uses pooled mosquitoes (10–50 per pool) and visualizes viral families detected as sequence-read heatmaps, while dust studies include clinic waiting-area air sampling and comparative dust sources (Phoenix garden dust, indoor robotic vacuum dust, passive air sampler dust) visualized in a treemap. A third line of work examines how environmental factors influence dust—and potentially virus—accumulation on phones/tablets, with ongoing metagenomic sequencing experiments under controlled conditions.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Use unbiased metagenomics sequencing and bioinformatic analyses to track viruses across different environmental sample matrices (dust/air, mosquitoes) and use viral genome sequences to infer evolutionary history and transmission routes.

Approach: Population: * Mosquito pools (10–50 mosquitoes per pool sample). Primary outcome: Virus detection profiles by family across matrices; comparative patterns by mosquito species and dust source; dust accumulation under varying environmental conditions.
Finding: Metagenomics + bioinformatics can track viruses across dust/air and mosquito matrices; viral genomes can inform evolutionary history and transmission routes.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Viral genomes detected via unbiased metagenomics in diverse matrices can inform evolutionary history and transmission routes; three applied settings illustrate the approach (mosquito surveillance, dust virome profiling, and device dust dynamics). Implications (poster-grounded): Environmental surveillance across vectors and dust/air could enhance situational awareness of viral circulation and potential transmission routes. Understanding dust accumulation dynamics on devices may identify overlooked pathways for pathogen carriage in daily environments (the poster frames devices as dust/virus accumulation sites). Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Dust can carry pathogens and facilitate environmental transmission; small particles can reach lower respiratory tract. Dust accumulation influenced by temperature, humidity, AC/DC field, and surface material. Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Prior work showed comparable virus profiles in chicken feces and dust from the same farms.
key_numbers: Not stated on poster for number of pools/samples; mosquito pool size is stated (10–50).
* Mosquito pools (10–50 mosquitoes per pool sample).
Mosquito pools (10–50 per pool) show virus-family patterns that vary by mosquito species (heatmap uses reads >1000 nt).
Dust can carry pathogens; small dust particles (≤10 microns) can reach the lower respiratory tract.
Treemap compares kingdom-level composition (eukaryote/bacteria/virus) across garden, vacuum, and passive air sampler dust (reads >1000 nt).
The mosquito surveillance work (in collaboration with Maricopa County Vector Control and ASU collaborators) uses pooled mosquitoes (10–50 per pool) and visualizes viral families...
takeaways: Metagenomics + bioinformatics can track viruses across dust/air and mosquito matrices; viral genomes can inform evolutionary history and transmission routes.
Mosquitoes are vectors for serious human viruses (examples: West Nile virus, Zika virus); poster states “There is currently no vaccine and no effective treatments.” (no further specifics provided).
Mosquito pools show virus-family patterns that vary by mosquito species (heatmap with colored boxes highlighting patterns).
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What metagenomic protocols and bioinformatic pipelines were used (library prep, sequencing platform, host read removal, classifier, database)?

what_to_ask.question: In Fig 1, which virus families dominate across mosquito pools, and which appear species-specific (as highlighted by colored boxes)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How do you confirm that detected viral reads represent true sample signal rather than index hopping or lab contamination?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: How could Maricopa County vector control use these data operationally (early warning, targeting interventions)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
